title: Are plant based substitutes healthier than meat

segments.text: Hello, this is Six Minute English from BBC Learning English, I'm Neil.
And I'm Georgie. If you've heard about meat-free Mondays or
Veganary, you probably know about a trend which an increasing number of people are trying
to reduce or even stop eating meat. This is often for health, animal welfare or environmental
reasons. Instead, people are getting their daily amount
of protein through plant-based alternatives. Meat-free sausages and burgers made using
ingredients like soy, tofu or fungi, as well as temper, beans or nuts.
Have you tried any of these plant-based meat alternatives, Georgie?
Yes, I have. I have lots of vegetarian friends, so I've been to veggie barbecues and have
tried the plant-based sausages and burgers. I quite like them, as long as they're covered
in sauce. What about you?
Yeah, I think I agree. They're fine. There's a reason for them, but I'm not sure they're
as good as the real thing.
Now with processed meat being linked to diseases like diabetes and cancer, most doctors agree
that plant-based options are healthier. But some people are worried about how plant-based
alternatives are made, including radio listener Graham Glover, who asked this question to BBC
Radio 4 program, sliced bread.
My question is, looking at these vegetable sausages, how they are processed for the additives
and flavorings and colorings, are they any healthier than a pork sausage?
In this episode, we'll be finding out whether plant-based foods really are a healthier alternative
to meat. And as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases. Remember,
you can find all the vocabulary along with a quiz and a worksheet on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
But now I have a question for you, Georgie. Listener Graham loves eating sausages. But
do you know which of the following words means sausages in British English slang? Is it
A, booze, B, banger, or C, brunch?
So I actually know the answer to this question, so I'm not going to say otherwise it will
spoil it for our listeners.
Okay, well, we'll all find out at the end of the program.
Although most research agrees that plant-based foods are healthier than meat, it's also
true that meat-free products are processed. To make meat-free sausages, for example,
after the proteins are broken down, flavorings and colorings are added to reproduce the taste
or texture of meat. Corn, one of the best-known brands of meat-free products, uses mycoprotein,
a fungi-based protein similar to the fungi used in the fermentation of yogurt and bread.
So what does listener Graham think? Let's find out as he talks to Greg Foote, presenter
of BBC Radio 4's Slice Bread.
Graham, listening to all this, you know, normally a fan of a meat sausage, your plant-based
meat alternative sausage curious that we're exploring today. What do you think about
hearing about the sources of where they come from? What's your gut reaction to hearing
that corn comes from a fungus?
I have no problems with that. It's just another form of protein, as far as I'm concerned.
It's what's the healthiest.
Yes, we will get to health. I think that's the big conversation for today.
Greg asks Graham for his gut reaction. Your gut reaction is your opinion based on your
initial feeling or instinct about something. It can't be explained logically.
Actually, Graham doesn't mind that his sausage protein is a fungus, as long as it's healthy.
He uses the phrase "as far as I am concerned" to indicate that he is giving his own opinion
on the subject.
Of course, healthy eating is important, but there are other factors influencing whether
meat lovers like Graham could be persuaded to try plant-based alternatives. These include
things like the taste, color and texture of the sausage. In other words, whether a plant-based
sausage looks and feels like real meat. Here's Greg discussing this point for BBC Radio
4 program, Slice to Bread.
It's interesting, isn't it, because my mum is a long-time veggie, and we were talking
about this, and she says, "I don't want my, you know, alternative proteins to resemble
meat." That's one of the reasons she says she doesn't eat meat, but you, Graham, you
said you do want your plant-based sausage to resemble a pork sausage.
Yeah, I've obviously been a meat eater, but I do need a plant-based product to be similar
in terms of flavour, texture and appearance.
Graham wants his plant-based sausages to resemble, meaning "looks similar to meat," but that's
not the case for Greg's mum, who's a long-time veggie. A veggie is slang for a vegetarian,
a person who doesn't eat meat. And the adjective "long time" describes someone who has been
or done a particular thing for many years, so a long time veggie is someone who's been
vegetarian for many years.
Whether you want your veggie sausages to look, taste and feel like meat, or you prefer your
protein from a plant, now there's every chance you'll find what you're looking for.
And keep your body and planet healthy as well.
Now isn't it time you revealed the answer to your question, Neil?
Yes, I asked what the British English slang word for sausages is, and what is it, Georgie?
The answer is "banger" and we have a typical dish in the UK called "bangers and mash",
which is "sausages and mash".
Mmm, love it, with gravy.
Okay, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with "banger", a word which is slang
for sausage.
Your gut reaction is your opinion about something based on instinctive feelings, not logic
or reason.
The phrase "as far as I'm concerned" can be used to give your opinion about something.
To resemble means to look or be similar to something.
A veggie is slang for a vegetarian, someone who doesn't eat meat.
And finally, the adjective "long time" can be used to say that someone has done a particular
thing for many years.
Once again our six minutes are up, but remember you can find worksheets, quizzes and loads
more resources to improve your English over on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
See you there soon.
But for now, goodbye.
Goodbye.
